A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[Axe]]s **[[Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[blacksmithing]], [[bead]] making, and the production of [[earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[Axe]]s **[[Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[blacksmithing]], [[bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[Axe]]s **[[Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * [https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html Muki-Banda Remains] * [http://www.yonago-navi.jp/en/yonago/yodoe/sightseeing/mukibanda-remains/ Mukibanda Historical Site, Yonago Tourist Site] * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221 妻木晩田遺跡 史跡名勝記念物]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/663092/20120309135217377.pdf Visitors' brochure]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.kanku-city.or.jp/yayoi/ 大阪府立弥生文化博物館 (Museum of Yayoi Culture, Osaka)]{{in lang|ja}}

A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[Axe]]s **[[Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[Axe]]s **[[Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * [https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html Muki-Banda Remains] * [http://www.yonago-navi.jp/en/yonago/yodoe/sightseeing/mukibanda-remains/ Mukibanda Historical Site, Yonago Tourist Site] * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221 妻木晩田遺跡 史跡名勝記念物]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/663092/20120309135217377.pdf Visitors' brochure]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.kanku-city.or.jp/yayoi/ 大阪府立弥生文化博物館 (Museum of Yayoi Culture, Osaka)]{{in lang|ja}}

The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * [https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html Muki-Banda Remains] * [http://www.yonago-navi.jp/en/yonago/yodoe/sightseeing/mukibanda-remains/ Mukibanda Historical Site, Yonago Tourist Site] * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221 妻木晩田遺跡 史跡名勝記念物]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/663092/20120309135217377.pdf Visitors' brochure]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.kanku-city.or.jp/yayoi/ 大阪府立弥生文化博物館 (Museum of Yayoi Culture, Osaka)]{{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[:en:Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[:en:cornerstone|cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[:en:Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[:en:cornerstone|cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[:en:Civil War of Wa|Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * [https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html Muki-Banda Remains] * [http://www.yonago-navi.jp/en/yonago/yodoe/sightseeing/mukibanda-remains/ Mukibanda Historical Site, Yonago Tourist Site] * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221 妻木晩田遺跡 史跡名勝記念物]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/663092/20120309135217377.pdf Visitors' brochure]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.kanku-city.or.jp/yayoi/ 大阪府立弥生文化博物館 (Museum of Yayoi Culture, Osaka)]{{in lang|ja}}

The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[:en:Civil War of Wa|Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[:en:Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[:en:golf course|golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[:en:Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[:en:cornerstone|cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[:en:Civil War of Wa|Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[Iron]]ware, including: **[[:en:Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site | native_name = 妻木晩田遺跡 | native_name_lang = | image = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area-1.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Mukibanda Site, Mukiyama area | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mukibanda ruins | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E|region:JP-31|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | region = [[San'in region]] | type = settlement ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = first to second centuries CE | abandoned = | epochs = [[Yayoi period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (museum) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=16|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|36|N|133|26|51|E}}}}}}
{{Nihongo|'''Mukibanda Yayoi Settlement Site'''|妻木晩田遺跡|Mukibanda-iseki}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] with a large [[Yayoi period]] settlement remains, straddling the border between the municipalities of [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]] and [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]] in the [[San'in region]] of western [[Japan]].<ref name="explore">{{Cite web |url=http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |title=Muki-Banda Remains |access-date=2012-04-07 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html |archive-date=2012-09-04 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The Mukibanda site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1999.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221|title= 妻木晩田遺跡|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=January 2, 2023}}</ref>
==General description== The Mukibanda site is located in the low [[:en:foothill|foothill]]s of [[Mount Daisen]],<ref name="chimei">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 | title = Mukibanda-iseki (妻木晩田遺跡) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-07 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | date = | archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archivedate = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref> at an elevation of between {{Convert|90|m|ft}} and {{Convert|120|m|ft}} above [[sea level]], and covers {{Convert|170|ha|acre}}. The settlement was naturally protected by the foothills of Mount Daisen, yet had close access to [[Miho Bay]] on the [[Sea of Japan]], which is clearly visible from the site.<ref name="chimei"/> It is divided into several discreet areas along the hill ridge. Continuing excavations have confirmed the remains of more than 420 pit dwellings, more than 500 pillar-supported buildings, and more than 30 Kofuns. It is estimated that there are more than 1,000 buildings including unexplored areas, making it one of the largest settlements in the Yayoi period.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Discovery== Construction of a [[:en:golf course|golf course]] and large-scale "Daisen Swiss Village" resort was planned on the site in the early 1990s by the [[Keihan Electric Railway|Keihan Group]], but after an examination of the area by the Boards of Education of Daisen and [[Yodoe, Tottori|Yodoe]], now Yonago City, between 1995 and 1998, a national-level conservation movement sought to protect the area from development.<ref name="chimei"/> The ruins cover an area of 156 hectares.
===Districts=== The Mukibanda Yayoi remains are divided into seven districts.
*Sentani *Muki Niiyama *Mukiyama *Matsuogashira *Komaishi *Shimizu *Matsuojo<ref name="explore"/>
==Excavation== Roughly 1/20th of the Mukibanda remains have been excavated.<ref name="towa">[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=42521 妻木晩田遺跡とは?]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The {{Convert|17.2|ha|acre}} of excavation revealed 395 [[:en:Dugout (shelter)|pit-style dwellings]], 502 dwellings with raised [[:en:cornerstone|cornerstone]]s, and 24 Yayoi-style barrow cliff tombs.<ref name="chimei"/> This area was inhabited between the late Yayoi to early [[Kofun period]], roughly 100 BC to 300 AD.<ref name="towa"/> The east part of the site was occupied by dwellings, and the western part of the site, on higher ground, was used for gravesites. The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[:en:Civil War of Wa|Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[:en:Iron|Iron]]ware, including: **[[:en:Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[:en:Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * [https://archive.today/20120904072652/http://www.japan-i.jp/explorejapan/chugoku/tottori/yonago-daisen/d8jk7l000002t1q0.html Muki-Banda Remains] * [http://www.yonago-navi.jp/en/yonago/yodoe/sightseeing/mukibanda-remains/ Mukibanda Historical Site, Yonago Tourist Site] * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/3221 妻木晩田遺跡 史跡名勝記念物]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/663092/20120309135217377.pdf Visitors' brochure]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.kanku-city.or.jp/yayoi/ 大阪府立弥生文化博物館 (Museum of Yayoi Culture, Osaka)]{{in lang|ja}}

The settlement was active in [[:en:blacksmithing|blacksmithing]], [[:en:Bead|Bead]] making, and the production of [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] [[Yayoi pottery]]. The highest point on the site in the Matsuogashira district of Daisen appears to be the home of the chief of the village and home to the sacred area of the site.<ref name="chimei"/> The entire site is thought to be the chief village, and possibly capitol, of some type of political entity.<ref name="towa"/> The Mukibanda site is likely part of ancient [[:en:Izumo Province|Kingdom of Izumo]]<ref>[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/explanation.asp 史跡名勝天然記念物：妻木晩田遺跡 詳細解説]{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The existence of ring trenches dating from the latter half of the Yayoi period indicate that the settlement was fortified at some point, roughly corresponding to the time of the [[:en:Civil War of Wa|Civil War of Wa]] mentioned in Chinese historical sources. Remains unearthed include earthenware, stone tools (cooking tools, agricultural tools, hunting tools, weapons), ironware (agricultural tools, weapons), and broken mirrors. A total of 197 ironware items from the Yayoi period, such as hatchets, axes, chisels, drilling tools, hoe tips, sickles, and iron arrowheads, have been excavated, and some of them are of continental origin.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Remains=== *[[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] *[[Stoneware]], including: **[[:en:Cookware|Cookware]] **Agricultural implements **Hunting implements **Weapons *[[:en:Iron|Iron]]ware, including: **[[:en:Hoe (tool)|Hoes]] **[[Spade]]s **[[Plow]]s **[[Sickle]]s **[[:en:Axe|Axe]]s **[[:en:Chisel|Chisel]]s **Awls *Weapons *Mirror fragments<ref name="chimei"/>
==Visiting the site== The Mukibanda remains are open to the public. Tours, demonstrations, special events, and reconstructions at the site can be seen throughout the year.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41867 むきばんだ史跡公園のイベントカレンダー]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
===Access=== The Mukibanda remains are closest to the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]] [[Yonago Station]] (15 minute bus ride), but is also accessible from the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[San'in Main Line|San'in Main]] and [[:en:Inbi Line|Inbi]] lines at [[Tottori Station]] (2 hour bus ride). The site is accessible by road via the [[San'in Expressway]], [[Japan National Route 431]], and [[Japan National Route 9]].<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=41864 むきばんだ史跡公園のアクセス]{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
==Gallery==
<gallery> image:Mukianda remains four corners projection grave.jpg|Protected grave image:Mukibanda remains excavation spot at Mukiniiyama area.jpg|Mukiniiyama area image:Mukibanda remains house trace in Mukiyama area.jpg|Remains of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains inside of reconstruction Yayoi house.jpg|Reconstruction of a dwelling image:Mukibanda remains western hill at Donohara area.jpg|Donohara area </gallery>
==See also== * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Tottori)]] *[[Yayoi period]]
==References== {{reflist}}
